MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares to a strong set of recent and relevant baselines (Dcoref; Durrett & Klein 2013/2014; Martschat & Strube 2015; Clark & Manning 2015) and reports results on the same evaluation metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4) and datasets (WCR and OntoNotes). They also incorporate their classifier into Dcoref to demonstrate end-to-end gains and explicitly acknowledge dataset-size and precision/recall limitations. Related work coverage is described as adequate, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant prior work. | B(5): The paper compares to multiple appropriate and recent baselines (Dcoref; Durrett & Klein 2013/2014; Martschat & Strube 2015; Clark & Manning 2015) and evaluates on standard datasets (WCR and OntoNotes/CoNLL 2012) using standard metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4), indicating fair, like-for-like comparisons. It also integrates its classifier into Dcoref and reports the resulting change in overall coreference F1, discusses dataset size limitations (WCR smaller) and differences in precision vs recall, and the paper’s related-work coverage is described as adequate, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a strong set of recent and relevant baselines (Dcoref; Durrett & Klein 2013/2014; Martschat & Strube 2015; Clark & Manning 2015) and reports results on the same evaluation metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4) and datasets (WCR and OntoNotes). They also incorporate their classifier into Dcoref to demonstrate end-to-end gains and explicitly acknowledge dataset-size and precision/recall limitations. Related work coverage is described as adequate, indicating comprehensive and fair comparisons with relevant prior work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to multiple appropriate and recent baselines (Dcoref; Durrett & Klein 2013/2014; Martschat & Strube 2015; Clark & Manning 2015) and evaluates on standard datasets (WCR and OntoNotes/CoNLL 2012) using standard metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4), indicating fair, like-for-like comparisons. It also integrates its classifier into Dcoref and reports the resulting change in overall coreference F1, discusses dataset size limitations (WCR smaller) and differences in precision vs recall, and the paper’s related-work coverage is described as adequate, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper frames a focused subproblem (main-concept resolution in Wikipedia) and leverages Wikipedia markup and Freebase-derived features to tackle it, which is a useful and somewhat novel task formulation compared to general coreference. However, the modeling approach (hundreds of features and two linear-kernel SVMs, plus use as a high-precision sieve in Dcoref) is a fairly incremental application of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new method. The work clearly differentiates itself from prior coreference systems and demonstrates measurable gains, so it represents moderate novelty rather than a breakthrough. | B(4): The paper frames and targets a specific subtask—main-concept resolution in Wikipedia—and presents a dedicated classifier and a high-precision sieve integrated into Dcoref, which constitutes a notable new perspective beyond general coreference work. It clearly differentiates itself by exploiting Wikipedia markup and Freebase-derived features, training separate pronominal and non-pronominal SVMs, and demonstrating large empirical gains (89% F1 on the task; +13 F1 over baselines; +4 points when added to Dcoref). The techniques themselves (feature engineering plus linear SVMs) are standard, so the contribution is more in problem formulation, feature/KB exploitation, and system integration than in novel algorithms.

The work clearly differentiates itself from prior coreference systems and demonstrates measurable gains, so it represents moderate novelty rather than a breakthrough.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it addresses coreference resolution (main-concept resolution) and evaluates on standard datasets (OntoNotes/CoNLL) using established metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4) and strong baselines (Dcoref, Durrett & Klein, Clark & Manning). The paper targets the right audience by proposing an applied resolution task for Wikipedia, releasing datasets/predictions, and integrating the classifier as a high-precision sieve into Dcoref to improve overall coreference F1. The problem formulation, methods (feature-rich mention representations, SVM classifiers), and evaluation standards match expectations for a top venue, and the authors position the work relative to prior art while noting dataset size limitations. | B(5): The paper addresses coreference resolution — a central NLP problem — and evaluates on standard benchmarks (OntoNotes/CoNLL F1) while comparing to well-known baselines (Dcoref, Durrett & Klein, Clark & Manning), so the topic clearly falls within top-tier NLP/ML venues. The methodology (feature-rich classifiers using Wikipedia/Freebase-derived representations and integration as a high-precision sieve into Dcoref) and publicly released datasets are directly relevant to the venue’s audience and evaluation standards. Although the WCR dataset is smaller, the mixture of standard datasets, established metrics, and clear impact on coreference systems makes the work a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

The paper targets the right audience by proposing an applied resolution task for Wikipedia, releasing datasets/predictions, and integrating the classifier as a high-precision sieve into Dcoref to improve overall coreference F1. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): This work addresses a focused but meaningful problem—identifying mentions that corefer to a Wikipedia article's main concept—and demonstrates strong task-level gains (89% F1, an absolute +13 F1 over the best baseline). Its practical significance is increased by showing that the classifier can be used as a high-precision sieve to improve a general coreference system (Dcoref) by about 4 absolute CoNLL F1 points and by releasing datasets and predicted mentions for reuse. However, the contribution is somewhat narrow in scope (task-specific to Wikipedia main-concept resolution) and evaluation relies on a relatively small WCR dataset, limiting immediate transformative impact across the broader coreference field. | B(4): The paper addresses a focused but important problem (identifying mentions coreferent with a Wikipedia article's main concept) and reports strong empirical gains (89% F1, an absolute +13 F1 over the best baseline). Its classifier also yields a meaningful downstream improvement when added as a high-precision sieve to Dcoref (+4 percentage points overall coreference F1), and the use of Wikipedia/Freebase signals plus publicly released data increases practical reuse. The impact is somewhat tempered by the smaller WCR dataset and gains skewed toward precision rather than recall, so the work is likely to have strong but not transformative influence.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses a focused but meaningful problem—identifying mentions that corefer to a Wikipedia article's main concept—and demonstrates strong task-level gains (89% F1, an absolute +13 F1 over the best baseline). However, the contribution is somewhat narrow in scope (task-specific to Wikipedia main-concept resolution) and evaluation relies on a relatively small WCR dataset, limiting immediate transformative impact across the broader coreference field.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: the model is evaluated on WCR and OntoNotes (CoNLL 2012 test), compared against multiple strong baselines, and shown to improve overall coreference when added as a sieve to Dcoref. The paper reports hyperparameters and releases datasets and predicted mentions, and it acknowledges limitations (WCR small, precision-focused gains). However, the summary does not mention any ablation or sensitivity analyses of the many features or classifiers, and code release is not stated, leaving some reproducibility and analysis gaps. | B(4): Experiments are strong: evaluation on both WCR and OntoNotes and comparisons to multiple strong baselines (Dcoref, Durrett & Klein, Cort, Scoref) with CoNLL/MUC/B3/CEAF metrics and reported gains (89% F1, +13 F1 over best baseline; +4 points when integrated into Dcoref). The paper reports hyperparameters and publicly releases datasets and predicted mentions, and discusses some limitations (small WCR size; precision-heavy gains). However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are mentioned for the large feature set or the sieve integration, and code release is not stated, so reproducibility and analysis of feature contributions are only moderately addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are reasonably comprehensive: the model is evaluated on WCR and OntoNotes (CoNLL 2012 test), compared against multiple strong baselines, and shown to improve overall coreference when added as a sieve to Dcoref. The paper reports hyperparameters and releases datasets and predicted mentions, and it acknowledges limitations (WCR small, precision-focused gains). However, the summary does not mention any ablation or sensitivity analyses of the many features or classifiers, and code release is not stated, leaving some reproducibility and analysis gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments are strong: evaluation on both WCR and OntoNotes and comparisons to multiple strong baselines (Dcoref, Durrett & Klein, Cort, Scoref) with CoNLL/MUC/B3/CEAF metrics and reported gains (89% F1, +13 F1 over best baseline; +4 points when integrated into Dcoref). The paper reports hyperparameters and publicly releases datasets and predicted mentions, and discusses some limitations (small WCR size; precision-heavy gains). However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are mentioned for the large feature set or the sieve integration, and code release is not stated, so reproducibility and analysis of feature contributions are only moderately addressed.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports hyperparameters and training details (hyperparameters reported true), uses publicly available datasets (OntoNotes) and states that the authors publicly release their WCR datasets and predicted mentions, and evaluation uses standard, well-defined CoNLL/MUC/B3/CEAFφ4 metrics. However, code is not mentioned and the method relies on hundreds of engineered features derived from Wikipedia and Freebase (which may not be exhaustively enumerated), so exact reproduction would require some additional engineering. Overall, mostly reproducible with minor details missing. | B(4): Hyperparameters and training details are reported and evaluation metrics/procedures are standard and explicitly listed (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4). The authors publicly release their datasets and predicted-mention dump and describe using Wikipedia markup and Freebase to derive features, but they extract “hundreds” of features and code is not mentioned, so exact feature engineering and preprocessing steps are not fully specified. Thus the work is mostly reproducible from the paper but some important implementation and preprocessing details are likely missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and training details (hyperparameters reported true), uses publicly available datasets (OntoNotes) and states that the authors publicly release their WCR datasets and predicted mentions, and evaluation uses standard, well-defined CoNLL/MUC/B3/CEAFφ4 metrics. However, code is not mentioned and the method relies on hundreds of engineered features derived from Wikipedia and Freebase (which may not be exhaustively enumerated), so exact reproduction would require some additional engineering. Overall, mostly reproducible with minor details missing.

reviewer_b.justification: Hyperparameters and training details are reported and evaluation metrics/procedures are standard and explicitly listed (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4). The authors publicly release their datasets and predicted-mention dump and describe using Wikipedia markup and Freebase to derive features, but they extract “hundreds” of features and code is not mentioned, so exact feature engineering and preprocessing steps are not fully specified. Thus the work is mostly reproducible from the paper but some important implementation and preprocessing details are likely missing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: they report an 89% F1 for main-concept resolution (a +13 F1 absolute gain over the best baseline) and a +4-point absolute improvement when adding their high-precision sieve to Dcoref, using standard CoNLL metrics and appropriate baselines. The method (separate SVMs for pronominal/non-pronominal mentions with many Wikipedia/Freebase-derived features) and hyperparameter reporting indicate a sound experimental setup. Minor concerns remain: the primary WCR dataset is noted to be much smaller than OntoNotes (limiting generalizability), and no mention is made of statistical significance testing or public code release, which would strengthen reproducibility and confidence in the results. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear empirically supported: the paper reports an 89% F1 (an absolute +13 F1 over the best baseline) and a +4 point overall gain when adding the classifier as a sieve to Dcoref, and compares against appropriate strong baselines (Dcoref, Durrett & Klein, Clark & Manning) on standard metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4). Mathematical content is minimal and no derivations are required; the methodology (feature sets, separate SVMs for pronouns/non-pronouns, hyperparameters reported) is sound. Minor concerns remain: the WCR dataset is much smaller (limiting generality) and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or whether the 89% figure generalizes beyond WCR, and reliance on Wikipedia/Freebase features may restrict applicability. These are relatively small issues that do not invalidate the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: they report an 89% F1 for main-concept resolution (a +13 F1 absolute gain over the best baseline) and a +4-point absolute improvement when adding their high-precision sieve to Dcoref, using standard CoNLL metrics and appropriate baselines. The method (separate SVMs for pronominal/non-pronominal mentions with many Wikipedia/Freebase-derived features) and hyperparameter reporting indicate a sound experimental setup. Minor concerns remain: the primary WCR dataset is noted to be much smaller than OntoNotes (limiting generalizability), and no mention is made of statistical significance testing or public code release, which would strengthen reproducibility and confidence in the results.

Mathematical content is minimal and no derivations are required; the methodology (feature sets, separate SVMs for pronouns/non-pronouns, hyperparameters reported) is sound. Minor concerns remain: the WCR dataset is much smaller (limiting generality) and the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or whether the 89% figure generalizes beyond WCR, and reliance on Wikipedia/Freebase features may restrict applicability. These are relatively small issues that do not invalidate the main experimental conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and clearly presents its main contributions early (the main-concept classifier, the 89% F1 result, and the Dcoref++ integration) and follows with a coherent methods and experiments section describing feature sets and separate SVMs for pronominal/non-pronominal mentions. Notation and experimental details appear consistent and reproducible (hyperparameters reported, related work coverage adequate), and overall writing quality is described as good. A minor presentation concern is that the WCR evaluation set is much smaller than OntoNotes (noted by the authors) and the summary does not mention figures/tables, so there may be small gaps in visual presentation or illustrative examples. | B(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (they report an 89% F1 and describe adding a high-precision sieve to Dcoref). The methodology, datasets (WCR, OntoNotes), baselines, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameter reporting are all described, and the writing quality is noted as good with adequate related-work coverage. One minor presentation issue is that the WCR dataset size limitation is acknowledged, suggesting some room for clearer discussion of dataset impact, but overall the exposition is strong.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and clearly presents its main contributions early (the main-concept classifier, the 89% F1 result, and the Dcoref++ integration) and follows with a coherent methods and experiments section describing feature sets and separate SVMs for pronominal/non-pronominal mentions. Notation and experimental details appear consistent and reproducible (hyperparameters reported, related work coverage adequate), and overall writing quality is described as good. A minor presentation concern is that the WCR evaluation set is much smaller than OntoNotes (noted by the authors) and the summary does not mention figures/tables, so there may be small gaps in visual presentation or illustrative examples.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (they report an 89% F1 and describe adding a high-precision sieve to Dcoref). The methodology, datasets (WCR, OntoNotes), baselines, evaluation metrics, and hyperparameter reporting are all described, and the writing quality is noted as good with adequate related-work coverage. One minor presentation issue is that the WCR dataset size limitation is acknowledged, suggesting some room for clearer discussion of dataset impact, but overall the exposition is strong.

main_claims: They developed a classifier that accurately identifies all mentions that co-refer to the main concept described in a Wikipedia article.
Their model achieves 89% F1 on the task, an absolute gain of 13 F1 points over the best baseline.

Incorporating their classifier as a high-precision sieve into the Stanford deterministic system (Dcoref) yields a 4 percentage-point absolute improvement in overall coreference F1 on the evaluated data.
They exploit Wikipedia markup and links to external KBs (Freebase) to extract features and publicly release their datasets and a dump of predicted main-concept mentions.
_summary.methodology: They compute representations of mentions and the article's main concept using Wikipedia and Freebase-derived information, extract hundreds of features (separately for pronominal and non-pronominal mentions), and train two linear-kernel SVM classifiers (one for pronominal, one for non-pronominal mentions) to predict main-concept coreference; the classifier is also used as a high-precision sieve in Dcoref (Dcoref++).
limitations: The WCR dataset used for evaluation is much smaller than OntoNotes (the paper states WCR is far smaller).
The reported overall gain is larger in precision than in recall (the paper notes the gain in precision is larger than the one in recall).
